title: Throwing Errors 🧱
description: How to intentionally cause a crash.

slides.title: Intentional Explosions 🧨
slides.content:
Java intercepts unintentional errors (like dividing by zero).

But sometimes, YOU want to cause an explosion intentionally. 

If you write a Setter method `setAge(int newAge)`, and another developer tries to pass in `-500`, you don't just want to ignore it. You want to throw an Exception and scream at them!

slides.title: The 'throw' keyword 🧨
slides.content: You can manually create and hurl an Exception object using the **`throw`** keyword.

quizData.question: What is the purpose of the `throw` keyword?

options.text: To intentionally trigger an Exception, effectively halting the program if someone uses your method incorrectly.

quizData.explanation: Throw allows you to create your own boundaries. If a user tries to set an age of -500, throwing an `IllegalArgumentException` is the correct response.

slides.title: Building Robust Libraries 🛡️
slides.content:
Why intentionally crash?

Because quiet failures are the worst bugs in programming. If age is just quietly ignored when it's `-500`, the developer will spend days wondering why their code isn't working.

By throwing an Exception, you provide a glaring red error message telling them exactly what they did wrong. Then, they can wrap their call in a `try/catch` and handle it properly.
